echo (Energy and Commodity Holistic Optimiser)
  • »
  • Expansion planning
  • View page source

Expansion planning

Expansion planning refers to decisions about asset expansions (whether to build new assets), capacity expansions (the size of new assets, or the increased capacity of existing assets), and retirement planning (whether to retire assets). Costs can be applied to these decisions that reflect the cost of building new assets, increasing capacity of existing assets, and retiring assets.

Expansion periods

In echo, expansion planning is implemented using a planning interval, which is distinct from the time intervals over which operational decisions are made. For example, time intervals might be in 1 hr increments, while planning intervals might be in increments of years.

How it works

The user add must any potential future assets and installation costs to the network before optimisation. They therefore specify where in the network these assets might be located. They then must specify parameters such as:

  • Maximum number of new assets per planning period, or over the entire optimisation window

  • Maximum number of assets retired per planning period, or over the entire optimisation window

  • Whether the assets have fixed parameters (e.g., fixed storage capacity for a battery), or whether these attributes are able to be optimised against some cost per unit capacity.

Asset Expansions

Variables

The asset expansion variables belong to the node. They include:

\(installed\), binary variable indexed by planning periods, for indicating when the asset is installed

\(installed^{int}\), non negative integer variable indicating which period the asset is installed, if installed.

\(installed^{max}\), binary variable, indicating whether the asset got installed in any time period.

Constraints

Constraints include a pair of big M constraints to set the integer variable \(installed^{int}\) correctly:

\(installed^{int}_p \geq p - M\cdot installed_p + 1\) for p in planning periods.

\(installed^{int}_p \leq p + M\cdot (1- installed_p)\) for p in planning periods.

If the asset is installed in time period p, then the first constraint enforces that \(installed^{int}\) is greater than or equal to p. The second constraint enforces that \(installed^{int}\) is less than or equal to p. If the asset is never installed, the first constraint will set \(installed^{int}\) equal to the last planning period + 1.

A constraint for setting \(installed^{max}\):

\(installed \leq installed^{max}\)

And a pair of big M constraints to force all ports on the node to be zero before the node is installed. For each port value \(port\):

\(port_{p, t} \leq M\cdot \sum_{i=0}^{p+1}installed_{i}\)

\(port_{p, t} \geq - M\cdot \sum_{i=0}^{p+1}installed_{i}\)

The above constraints enforces that once \(installed\) is 1, each \(port\) is effectively unconstrained, but before \(installed\) is 1, \(port\) is forced to be zero.

Costs

The user can specify an install cost in $, which is a fixed cost that is applied if the asset is installed.

Capacity Expansion

Some assets have a capacity which can be optimised. Currently only storage capacity is supported.

The user can specify a capacity cost in $/unit capacity, which is added to the optimisation objective.

Asset Retirement

Assets have a lifetime that is defined in planning period intervals (e.g., years). At the end of their lifetime, assets must either be replaced or retired. Replacement resets the lifetime of the asset to its nominal lifetime, allowing the asset to continue to be operated. Retirement means that the asset ceases to operate for all subsequent expansion periods. Appropriate costs can be applied to retirement/replacement decisions.

Variables

The asset retirement variables belong to the node. They include:

\(retire\), binary variable indexed by planning periods, for indicating when we retire the asset

\(replace\), binary variable indexed by planning periods, indicating in which time period we replace the asset

\(life\), non negative variable, indicating how much remaining lifetime the asset has (in number of planning intervals)

Parameters

\(life^{nominal}\), the asset’s nominal lifetime, which is also the lifetime of any replacement

Constraints

Remaining lifetime rule:

\(life_{p} = life_{p-1} + replace_{p} \cdot (life^{nominal} + 1) + retire_{p} - 1\)

Permanent retirement rule:

\(retire_p >= retire_{p-1}\)

Force all ports on node to be zero after retirement using a pair of big M constraints: For each port value \(port\):

\(port_{p, t} \leq M\cdot (1 - retire_p)\)

\(port_{p, t} \geq - M\cdot (1 - retire_p)\)

Costs

The user can specify a replacement cost in $, which is applied every time an asset is replaced.
